title: Add hook leader-elector, checksum-based idempotency and migration dry-run values for private installer
preview: Motivation: on air-gapped and multi-operator installs we observed multiple init/postupgrade hooks racing which caused duplicate migrations and transient secret reconciliation failures. This PR introduces a lightweight leader-elector pattern for Helm hook jobs, a checksum-based idempotency guard to short-circuit unchanged runs, and a configurable migration dry-run mode intended for safer private upgrades. Summary of changes: 1) templates/_hook_helpers.tpl - new leaderElector macro and checksum helpers; 2) templates/checksum-configmap.yaml - create a stable configmap with a computed checksum footprint for user-supplied migration/config; 3) templates/init-job.yaml and templates/migration-job.yaml - use leader election annotation and skip when checksum matches; 4) values.schema.json and values.yaml - add migrate.dryRun, hooks.leaderElector.enabled, hooks.leaderElector.lockTTL, hooks.checksum.enabled and externalLoadBalancer.annotations passthrough; 5) tests/integration/migration-dryrun.sh - CI integration test that validates dry-run no-op behavior. Design notes: leader-elector is implemented as a conservative ConfigMap-based lock annotation (no extra controller) so it works in air-gapped clusters without additional CRDs; lock TTL is configurable and defaults to 10m to avoid long stuck locks in failed runs. Checksum logic ties to a canonical set of keys (config, secrets, job templates) so trivial template re-renders do not re-trigger heavy migrations. The migrate.dryRun flag runs schema validation and a noop path for database migrations and reports a JSON summary to stdout for operator review. Testing and rollout: unit and chart-lint added; integration migration-dryrun test runs in CI; deployment plan is to enable the feature behind values defaults (leaderElector.enabled = true, migrate.dryRun = false) and rollout to internal canary clusters first. Backwards compatibility: all hooks remain functional if feature flags are disabled. See linked Linear tickets for rollout and post-merge follow-ups.

title: helm: introduce cost-profiles chart with QuantConfig CRD and exporter
preview: Motivation: Provide a Helm-native way to ship operator-friendly defaults for batching, cache presets, and quantization profiles so platform deployers can opt into cost-optimization automation during chart install/upgrade. This PR adds a new 'cost-profiles' Helm chart that bundles a QuantConfig CRD, a lightweight exporter sidecar that emits curated presets to the control-plane API, and opinionated values for batching/caching defaults. Key features: 1) QuantConfig CRD: a cluster-scoped CRD model that encodes quantization tiers (fp16, int8, q4_0), acceptable accuracy degradation thresholds, and rollout tags; 2) exporter: a small Deployment (exporter sidecar) that reads installed CR instances and posts signed preset bundles to the Redwood control plane or dumps them to a configured S3 bucket; 3) values.yaml presets: safe-by-default batch sizes, KV cache TTLs, and host-level annotations to opt deployments into conservative or aggressive profiles; 4) upgrade path: chart registers/validates existing QuantConfig objects and runs a dry-run export during helm upgrade by default. Backwards compatibility: existing clusters that don't install this chart will be unaffected; the CRD is optional and installs as an opt-in. Security: exporter uses ephemeral service account with RBAC limited to reading the CRD and writing to a configured outbound endpoint; TLS and token-auth are enforced. Testing: unit templates tested via helm unittest; end-to-end smoke tests run in the staging cluster to ensure installer doesn't change existing deployments; we exercised the exporter against a local mock control-plane and an S3 sink; load tests verified default batch sizes do not increase median latency beyond the 95th-percent SLO for small models. Checklist: - [x] Add Chart scaffolding + README - [x] Implement QuantConfig CRD template and validation schema - [x] Add exporter manifest + ConfigMap-driven templates - [x] Values presets + examples for conservative/aggressive modes - [x] Unit tests (helm-unittest) - [x] E2E smoke in staging - [x] Security review and RBAC policy - [x] Docs entry and upgrade notes How to test locally: helm install ./cost-profiles --set exporter.sink=mock --namespace redwood-test; verify crd present with kubectl get quantconfigs.redwood.ai; create a QuantConfig and watch exporter logs. Release notes: Adds cost-profiles Helm chart that enables cluster operators to export curated batching/caching defaults and quantization presets to the Redwood control plane; opt-in CRD and exporter; no automatic application of presets to runtime without explicit operator approval. Migration notes: None required for existing installs; operators who adopt should review preset thresholds in values.yaml. Related: ENG-3421 (helm presets design), ENG-4277 (quant profile schema).

title: introduce-postupgrade-cleanup-hook-and-explicit-values-schema-for-private
preview:
Motivation: Private installs occasionally leave transient cluster objects (temp PVs, breadcrumb ConfigMaps) after upgrade paths that changed storage/retention policies. Operators need a safe, opt-in post-upgrade cleanup hook that can be enabled in values and validated by an explicit values.schema.json. This PR implements an optional post-upgrade cleanup Helm hook, adds a JSON schema for values validation, and refactors common helpers so init and post-upgrade hooks share templating primitives.

What I changed (summary): commits: 1) add values.schema.json and update values.yaml defaults; 2) introduce templates/hooks/post-upgrade-cleanup.yaml implementing a Job hook guarded by .Values.postUpgrade.cleanup.enabled; 3) refactor templates/_helpers.tpl to expose hook labels and common container/env helpers shared with init-job.yaml; 4) add a minimal integration test charts/private/tests/upgrade-scenario.sh that simulates a canary upgrade and asserts the cleanup job runs only when enabled; 5) small README update documenting the new flag and migration notes.

Key behaviors and decisions: - The cleanup hook is explicitly opt-in: values.postUpgrade.cleanup.enabled is false by default. When enabled the hook runs as a Helm post-upgrade hook with policy: hook-delete-policy:hook-succeeded. The job is namespaced and uses the same serviceAccount as the init-job when present. - The job is tolerant: uses exponential backoff and a --dry-run mode controlled via values.postUpgrade.cleanup.dryRun for safer rollouts. - To avoid breaking existing installs we added values.schema.json that marks all new keys as optional and provides deprecation notes for legacy top-level keys. This allows CI (helm lint --values) + custom validation to catch misconfigurations. - We refactored helpers into templates/_helpers.tpl to keep templates DRY and reduce divergence between init and post-upgrade hook logic.

Files changed (high-level): - charts/private/values.schema.json: explicit schema for postUpgrade.cleanup, initJob, and common hook controls. - charts/private/templates/hooks/post-upgrade-cleanup.yaml: CronJob->Job style post-upgrade hook (Job) that runs the cleanup container, reads retention rules from a ConfigMap and exits gracefully when nothing to clean. - charts/private/templates/init-job.yaml: small adjustments to use the shared helpers and to expose the existing sa name via helper. - charts/private/templates/_helpers.tpl: new helpers to render hook annotations, labels, and job container command composition. - charts/private/tests/upgrade-scenario.sh: basic e2e-ish script used in CI job input to validate that enabling flag causes job creation and successful completion. - charts/private/README.md: documentation for new values, examples to enable cleanup, and troubleshooting notes.

Review notes and conversation snapshot: - Diego Alvarez: requested stronger schema coverage for the dryRun field and suggested moving the default imagePullPolicy to values.global to match other charts. I addressed both and pushed a follow-up commit that updated values.schema.json and values.yaml. - Priya Natarajan: asked for an escape hatch when the cleanup job misbehaves (she requested a configurable backoffLimit and a toggle to restrict runtime). I added values.postUpgrade.cleanup.backoffLimit and a short note in README about controlling the job lifecycle. - Samir Gupta: ran the upgrade-scenario test in his dev cluster and reported a lint failure due to a missing helper; I fixed the _helpers.tpl order and re-ran CI.

CI: The pipeline originally failed one lint step due to a templating order (fixed in 2nd commit). Full CI run after fixes: helm/lint -> pass, unit/templates -> pass, integration/upgrade-scenario -> pass (the script runs in a mocked KinD job in CI and verifies job creation and logs).

Compatibility and migration: This is non-breaking by default; the cleanup job is disabled unless explicitly enabled. values.schema.json is additive and marks legacy keys as deprecated but not removed. Operators upgrading the chart can enable cleanup post-upgrade after confirming retention rules.

Testing performed: - Local helm template runs against k8s 1.26 and 1.27 manifests. - Integration script executed in CI with KinD cluster; tested both enabled and disabled states. - Manual run in a staging cluster to validate ServiceAccount permissions and RBAC scoping.

Merge outcome: Approved by reviewers after two rounds of feedback. CI green. Squash-merged into main on 2026-02-25 with a brief merge commit.

Notes for follow-ups: - Consider exposing a metrics endpoint for the cleanup job for observability (ENG-3820). - Add a safety-mode that requires a specific annotation on the release to permit cleanup in production environments.

title: decouple-init-configmap-and-introduce-tenant-rotation-operator-helpers
preview: Motivation: In private installs we need a small, immutable init ConfigMap consumed by several init jobs and a lightweight tenant-rotation helper that can be turned on/off independently. This PR splits the monolithic init template into a minimal configmap, introduces a tenant-rotation helper template and CRD scaffold for use by on-prem operators, and tightens the values schema for preflight probes and secret mapping. Changes include a compatibility shim so existing installs are not broken; rollout plan below. Checklist: - split chart/templates/init.yaml into configmap + init-job templates; - add charts/_helpers.tpl functions for secretName and rotationLabel; - add values.schema.json entries for preflight.probes and rotation.enabled; - add templates/tenant-rotation-crd.yaml (scaffold) and templates/tenant-rotation-job.yaml (opt-in); - update NOTES.txt and README with operator opt-in steps; - CI: extend helm lint and kubeconform checks; Related: linear ENG-1882 tracks followups for tenancy metrics and a subsequent operator binary. This description is intentionally single-line to fit PR page export conventions.

title: namespace-scoped init phases and per-tenant template overlay support for private installs
preview:
Motivation: Private installs need safer, tenant-scoped initialization sequences and a maintainable way to apply per-namespace template overrides without adding global init jobs or replicating chart variants. This PR introduces two orthogonal capabilities: 1) namespace-scoped init phases that run initPods/Jobs limited to the target namespace and sequenced using phase ordering and explicit readiness gates, and 2) a lightweight template-overlay mechanism (values.overlays) that lets operators supply per-namespace templating fragments (ConfigMap-style YAML) applied via an idempotent post-render hook.

Summary of changes: added namespace-scoped init controller helpers, augmented values schema with overlays and phase ordering, introduced a post-render overlay applier job and an associated dry-run mode, updated helper library templates to support overlay merging, and added unit tests and chart documentation.

Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked in ENG-1942 - [x] Values schema updated with validation - [x] Backwards-compatible by default (overlays disabled) - [x] Unit tests for overlay merge logic - [x] Added e2e smoke for namespace init sequencing in chart-tests CI

Commits: - 1a2b3c4: add values.overlays schema and docs - 2b3c4d5: implement overlay post-render hook and dry-run flag - 3c4d5e6: introduce namespace phase annotation and leader-election guard for init jobs - 4d5e6f7: tests + chart-tests e2e - 5e6f7g8: docs, changelog, and examples for private install templates

Files changed (high level): - charts/private/values.schema.json: add overlays, perNamespace phases and validation rules - charts/private/templates/_init_helpers.tpl: new helpers for namespacing and phase ordering - charts/private/templates/init-phase-job.yaml: new namespace-scoped Job CRD template with leader election guard - charts/private/templates/overlay-applier-job.yaml: post-render job for applying per-namespace overlays (dry-run supported) - charts/private/templates/NOTES.txt: doc snippets for enabling overlays and troubleshooting - tests/chart-tests/overlay-sequencing.sh: simple e2e check - docs/private/overlays.md: operational guide and examples - ci/chart-tests.yaml: add smoke job for namespace init sequencing

Implementation notes: - Values schema: values.overlays is an object mapping namespace names to an array of overlay fragments (strings containing YAML). Each fragment is merged in order using a conservative merge (scalars replaced, lists replaced unless marked mergeList: true). The schema is strict to avoid surprising types. - Init sequencing: each namespace init job receives annotations: redwood.io/init-phase and redwood.io/phase-ready-probe. A lightweight controller in the private-installer helm hooks orchestrates wait-for-phase-ready semantics using Kubernetes probe semantics and leader-election (ConfigMap lock) to avoid parallel init collisions across Helm upgrades. - Overlay applier: placed as a post-render Helm hook invoked conditionally (values.overlays.enabled). The applier has a --dry-run option that validates that rendered manifests would be syntactically merged and emits a report to a ConfigMap for operator inspection. It uses a deterministic merge order to keep rollbacks simple.

Review conversation (selected): - Liam Chen (reviewer): "Can we guarantee idempotency if overlay fragments change order between upgrades? Suggest adding stable sort by fragment name/hash before merge."

  Ava Martinez (author): "Good catch — implemented stable-hash sort by fragment id and added tests to ensure deterministic merges. See commit 2b3c4d5."

  Priya Nair (reviewer): "The leader election guard relies on ConfigMap locks in cluster scope — this was a concern for air-gapped installs where RBAC is locked down. Can we scope locks to the namespace?"

  Ava Martinez: "Updated to use namespace-scoped Lease objects when the target install mode is 'namespaced' and fall back to cluster-scoped locks only when explicitly configured. Documented the RBAC change in docs/private/overlays.md."

  Ethan Park (reviewer): "Please add more observability — per-namespace init phase metrics and an alerting hook if a phase stalls."

  Ava Martinez: "Added a Prometheus metric 'redwood_private_init_phase_duration_seconds' with labels namespace and phase and a simple alert recipe. Also extended NOTES.txt with common failure modes and diagnostics."

CI summary: - chart lint: pass - unit tests: pass - chart-tests (k8s 1.26 smoke): pass (overlay dry-run + namespace sequencing) - e2e extended: rerun once due to flake, passed on rerun.

Backwards compatibility: The new values keys are opt-in (values.overlays.enabled = false by default). Existing installs are unaffected. There is a small RBAC footprint change when overlays.enabled = true (namespace Lease permissions and potential ConfigMap read for dry-run reports) — documented in the release notes and the private installer upgrade guide.

Operational guidance: - To enable per-namespace overlays set values.overlays.enabled=true and populate values.overlays.namespaces.my-tenant with an ordered list of fragments. - Use overlays.dryRun=true to validate before applying; a ConfigMap 'private-overlays-dryrun-report' will contain the merge result and any validation errors. - For large fleets, prefer a centralized overlay repo and a templating preflight step in CI to avoid oversized Helm value blobs.

Merge: Squash-and-merge after last approval. Merged by Liam Chen on 2026-02-25.

title: conditional-init-job-and-hook-templating-to-support-idempotent-private-upgrades
preview: Problem / motivation: private deployments running older charts sometimes fail upgrades when init/rollback jobs are unguarded or when cluster topology (multi-az vs single-az) requires different init behavior. This caused a recent support case (SUP-122) where the init job re-ran on every helm upgrade and blocked subsequent hooks. Goals: 1) make init/rollback jobs idempotent and conditional based on values and cluster facts, 2) provide a clear values migration path for private customers upgrading from older installers, 3) keep helm hooks robust for both air-gapped on-prem installs and VPC installs, 4) add minimal integration tests to prevent regressions. What this PR changes: - values.schema.json: introduce new top-level keys private.init.runOnce (boolean), private.init.leaderElectionConfig (string), private.upgrade.mode ("canary"|"standard"), and private.hooks.enableRollback (boolean). The schema contains descriptions and default values to be surfaced in the installer UI. - templates/init-job.yaml: converts the previous unconditional Job -> a helm template that renders the Job only when .Values.private.enabled and .Values.private.init.runOnce are true OR when clusterTopology == "multi-az" (we detect via .Capabilities but allow manual override). The Job implements a lightweight idempotency check against a ConfigMap (key: redwood-private-init-leader) so only one node performs expensive migrations; the Job re-checks to avoid duplicate work on retries. - templates/hooks/preflight-crd-check.yaml: pre-install hook that verifies required CRDs exist (CRDs vary for on-prem). title: helm-hook-helpers-and-values-example-generator
preview: Context: Private deployments have accumulated ad-hoc hook conditionals across multiple charts (private-installer, private-core, private-db). This causes subtle behavior divergence during upgrades/rollbacks and bloats templates. This PR centralizes hook decision logic into a chart helper and ships a small values-example generator that emits curated values.yaml snippets used in docs and in chart-tests. Motivation: make hook behavior consistent (init/cron/pre/post hooks), simplify templating, and provide deterministic example payloads that can be baked into release notes and upgrade playbooks. Summary of changes: - Add templates/_helpers/hook_helpers.tpl: a single entrypoint to evaluate whether to render a hook and which annotations to attach based on values (canaryInit, rollbackPolicy, atomicFallback). - Replace duplicated conditional blocks in charts/private-installer/templates/init-job.yaml and charts/private-core/templates/preupgrade-cron.yaml with calls to the helper. - Add tooling/dev/generate-values-examples.py: small script that reads values.schema.json and values.yaml and generates a set of annotated example snippets for docs and chart-tests. - Add values/examples/ canary and standard examples and wire them into docs build. - Extend chart-testing workflow: run helm template with --execute for hook resources and validate that no unexpected initJobs are created when canaryInit=false. - Add chart test fixture for an upgrade path that verifies hooks run only when canary toggle and rollbackPolicy are enabled. - Add docs/helm/hooks.md with examples and a short troubleshooting section for common upgrade failures. High-level commits: commit 1: introduce hook_helpers.tpl and unit tests for template logic; commit 2: refactor init/pre/post hook templates to use helper; commit 3: add generate-values-examples.py and CI step; commit 4: add docs and chart-tests; commit 5: fix CI lint failures for template indentation and re-run chart-tests. Files changed (high level): charts/private-installer/templates/init-job.yaml; charts/private-core/templates/preupgrade-cron.yaml; charts/private-db/templates/postupgrade-hook.yaml; charts/_helpers.tpl (new); values/examples/canary.yaml; values/examples/standard.yaml; tooling/generate-values-examples.py; .github/workflows/chart-testing.yml (updated); docs/helm/hooks.md. Important implementation notes: - The helper intentionally returns a tuple-like object through named labels (renderHook, hookAnnotation) encoded as a small line-delimited string to avoid nested dicts in templating. - generate-values-examples.py is deliberately small (no external deps beyond pyyaml used in CI) so it can be run in CI and locally by engineers. - The hook helper preserves existing annotation keys used by the private installer to signal the controller to wait for job completion; behavior is unchanged unless canaryInit is toggled. Backwards compatibility: default behavior is unchanged. The canary_init_job toggle is opt-in in values.yaml and off by default. This PR is not a breaking change. Testing and CI: - Initial push failed chart-testing due to helm-lint complaining about blank lines in helper comment blocks; fixed in subsequent commit. - chart-testing now runs helm template with an added --execute arg to validate hooks render as expected. - Unit tests added for template helper using helm unittest. Review/QA notes: Please pay attention to the helper contract (inputs: .Values.hooks.canaryInit, .Values.hooks.rollbackPolicy, .Release.Revision) and edge cases where rollbackPolicy="atomic" with canaryInit=true should still fall back to a safe path. Docs: hooks.md includes migration guidance for operators upgrading from older installers and a troubleshooting checklist used by Support when customers see stuck upgrades.

title: charts: add failover-controller values + safe defaults (dry-run, approvals, rate limits)
preview:
## Summary
This PR introduces first-class Helm chart support for the regional failover automation controller. The goal is to make controlled enablement safe and consistent across staging and production, with explicit feature flags, conservative resource defaults, and guardrails that reduce risk during noisy-signal periods.

Key points:
- Adds a `failoverController` block to the platform chart values with an explicit `enabled: false` default.
- Supports dry-run mode configuration (compute and emit audit events without executing flips) as the default behavior when enabled in staging.
- Adds optional approval hooks configuration (require on-call ack prior to execution) for production safety.
- Adds rate limiting / anti-flap knobs (max actions per hour, hold-down/settle timers) exposed as values and wired into container env/config.
- Adds recommended pod safety defaults: requests/limits, PDB, topology spread constraints, and default non-root securityContext.
- Adds common observability toggles for metrics/annotations and log format (structured json).

## Why
As part of the Reliability Program’s regional failover automation upgrade, we need a reliable and repeatable way to deploy the controller across environments with minimal risk. We also want the rollout plan to be “values-driven” so we can enable per-cluster/per-tenant behavior without chart patching.

## Implementation notes
- New template `templates/failover-controller/deployment.yaml` is gated behind `.Values.failoverController.enabled`.
- `dryRun` is a values switch (`.Values.failoverController.mode: dry-run|active`) rather than a separate chart to reduce drift between staging/prod.
- Approval hooks are disabled by default and only take effect when `.Values.failoverController.approvals.enabled=true`.
- Rate limiting defaults are conservative to reduce oscillation during partial impairments:
  - `maxActionsPerHour: 2`
  - `minSecondsBetweenActions: 600`
  - `holdDownSeconds: 900`
- Resources default to low but non-trivial to avoid CPU starvation during high event volume:
  - requests: cpu 250m / memory 512Mi
  - limits: cpu 1000m / memory 1Gi
- Adds PDB with `minAvailable: 1` to avoid voluntary disruption taking down the controller.

## Values added (high level)
- `failoverController.enabled`
- `failoverController.image.repository/tag/pullPolicy`
- `failoverController.mode` (default: `dry-run` when enabled; chart default remains disabled)
- `failoverController.approvals.enabled` + webhook/timeout
- `failoverController.rateLimits.maxActionsPerHour`, `minSecondsBetweenActions`
- `failoverController.timers.holdDownSeconds`, `signalStabilitySeconds`
- `failoverController.resources.requests/limits`
- `failoverController.podDisruptionBudget.enabled/minAvailable`
- `failoverController.podAnnotations` (for metrics scraping + rollout markers)
- `failoverController.extraEnv` and `extraArgs`

## Changed areas
- charts/redwood-platform/values.yaml
- charts/redwood-platform/values.schema.json
- charts/redwood-platform/templates/failover-controller/deployment.yaml
- charts/redwood-platform/templates/failover-controller/serviceaccount.yaml
- charts/redwood-platform/templates/failover-controller/pdb.yaml
- charts/redwood-platform/templates/_helpers.tpl (naming/labels helpers)
- charts/redwood-platform/README.md (document values + examples)

## Example values (staging)
Set:
- `failoverController.enabled: true`
- `failoverController.mode: dry-run`
- `failoverController.approvals.enabled: false`
- `failoverController.rateLimits.maxActionsPerHour: 10` (staging only)

## Testing
- `helm lint` on redwood-platform
- `helm template` validated for enabled/disabled paths
- CI renders manifests for both `mode=dry-run` and `mode=active` in matrix

## Rollout plan
- Land chart changes first (this PR)
- Enable in staging with `mode=dry-run`
- Promote to production with `enabled=true`, `mode=dry-run`, approvals enabled
- Once signal quality is validated via game day + burn rate stability, flip to `mode=active` per cluster with existing change approval workflow

Linked: ENG-2415 (dry-run), ENG-2416 (rollback guardrails), ENG-2411 (umbrella).

title: Expose optimization config version pinning via Helm values + document rollback procedure
preview: Summary: This PR adds first-class Helm values to pin the active Optimization Config version for Redwood Private installs and documents an operator-friendly rollback flow (pin to prior version + apply). Motivation: In Hosted/Dedicated, the control plane manages versioning and rollback; for Private, operators need a deterministic way to (a) hold a known-good version during upgrades and (b) roll back quickly when an optimization change causes a latency/5xx regression. This change introduces an explicit chart-level mechanism to pass the pinned version to the control plane/runtime, making the procedure consistent with the Safe Apply + Rollback project. What’s included: (1) New values: optimize.optimizationConfig.versionPin (string, optional) and optimize.optimizationConfig.allowUnpinned (bool, default true). When versionPin is set, the deployment will request that version from the Optimization Config Service and treat it as the active version for the configured scope. (2) Templating: inject OPTIMIZATION_CONFIG_VERSION_PIN and OPTIMIZATION_CONFIG_ALLOW_UNPINNED into the control-plane and runtime components; add checksum annotations so updating values triggers rollouts. (3) Validation: fail chart rendering when versionPin is set but empty/whitespace; add a warning note in NOTES.txt when allowUnpinned=false and no pin is supplied. (4) Docs: add a dedicated “Rollback optimization config” section with a copy-paste example (helm upgrade --set optimize.optimizationConfig.versionPin=<prior_version>) and post-rollback verification steps (check rollout status, confirm config_version tag on metrics, confirm p95 recovery). Backwards compatibility: Existing installs that do not set versionPin behave as before (control plane uses the currently-promoted version / unpinned resolution). No changes to defaults that would impact current clusters. Testing: helm lint, helm template, and a minimal kind-based smoke deploy in CI to ensure env vars are present and rollouts happen on pin changes. Follow-ups: (a) In a separate PR we can add a small “pin status” panel to the Private operator docs to align with Console wording, (b) add a values.schema.json pattern restriction once we finalize the canonical version ID format (currently treated as opaque string).

title: Add perf-canary Helm chart (safe defaults + region/env config)
preview:
Summary: This PR adds a first-class Helm chart for the perf-canary service, with conservative defaults intended for initial prod rollout (single region, small model set) and guardrails to prevent accidental wide fanout / excess GPU overhead. The chart supports per-environment and per-region configuration via values + explicit allowlists, and ships with security hardening defaults (non-root, RO filesystem, restricted SA by default) and observability integration hooks.

Context / why: perf-canary needs to be deployed consistently across regions with repeatable config (QPS caps, capacity budget, target models, label set). We also want a safe-disable/rollback path for on-call. This chart is the deployment substrate referenced by ENG-4879 and will be rolled out initially in us-east with a limited model list (ENG-4880).

Key behaviors / defaults:
- Disabled-by-default: chart does not run unless perfCanary.enabled=true.
- Single-region guardrail: requires region to be set explicitly; optionally enforces a regionAllowlist check.
- Capacity safety: defaults target <0.5% overhead via conservative qps + maxInflight limits (tunable).
- Pod hardening: runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, seccompProfile=RuntimeDefault, drop all caps, allowPrivilegeEscalation=false.
- Metrics: ServiceMonitor template (Prometheus Operator) + standard labels; histogram buckets controlled via values to avoid high cardinality.
- Rollback/disable: all resources are namespaced; disabling the release cleanly removes canary traffic generation without touching inference endpoints.

What’s included:
- charts/perf-canary (new): Chart.yaml, values.yaml, templates for Deployment, ServiceAccount, ConfigMap, NetworkPolicy, PDB, Service, ServiceMonitor, and helper templates.
- region/env configuration: values supports env, region, tier (hosted/dedicated/private), and an explicit model target list.
- secret wiring: supports referencing an existing secret for canary auth token (created out-of-band by terraform/IAM).

How to deploy (example): helm upgrade --install perf-canary charts/perf-canary -n runtime-canary --create-namespace -f values/prod/us-east.yaml --set perfCanary.enabled=true

Testing:
- chart-testing (ct) lint + install on kind
- helm lint + template render with representative prod values

Rollout notes:
- For phase 1: use us-east only, 2-3 models, warnings only. Paging is handled in observability-pack once thresholds are tuned.

Follow-ups:
- Add a small "values schema" (values.schema.json) for stronger validation and to prevent accidental enabling without region/model list.
- Wire default Grafana dashboard links into annotations once the dashboard UID is finalized.

title: Fix offline install values.schema.json validation (relax required fields + add defaults)
preview: Context: During the air-gapped pilots we hit schema validation failures on install/upgrade when using the “minimal offline” values files. The charts were technically installable, but values.schema.json was rejecting inputs due to (a) required fields that are optional in offline mode, and (b) missing defaults for fields that the templates already handle safely. This showed up most often when customers copy only the offline overrides from the docs/runbook (no hosted defaults), and when values are injected by the on-prem installer. title: Align chart versions and appVersion with Private offline bundle manifest metadata
preview:
Problem: For Redwood Private air-gapped installs, we now ship signed offline bundles where the bundle manifest is the source of truth for versioning. During pilot runs we hit cases where Chart.yaml appVersion drifted from the bundle manifest releaseVersion, which confused support triage and (worse) created ambiguity about what exactly was verified/signed. This PR makes the bundle manifest drive Helm chart versioning/appVersion consistently and adds a CI gate so we fail closed before publishing offline chart tarballs.

Context: Offline bundle signing effort (deterministic manifest + signature verification in installer) requires all user-facing version identifiers to be consistent across: (1) bundle manifest metadata, (2) chart metadata, (3) private release tooling outputs. This PR focuses on (2) and the glue in our chart packaging jobs.

What changed: - Introduced a small version stamping step that reads `private-bundle-manifest.json` (generated by release tooling) and stamps Chart.yaml fields: `version` and `appVersion` - Enforced that `appVersion` == `manifest.releaseVersion` (e.g., `2026.1.0`) across all Private-facing charts - Enforced that Chart `version` is derived deterministically from the manifest as well (we use `manifest.chartVersion`, which is produced by the release train with the same build id) - Updated our chart packaging pipeline to fail if the manifest is missing or if stamping would change a dirty working tree (prevents accidental publishing from non-deterministic state) - Updated chart-testing config to run `helm lint` after stamping so CI matches what we publish

Why this approach: - Keeps a single source of truth: the signed bundle manifest is already what the installer verifies. Chart metadata must match it, not the other way around. - Avoids manual edits at release time. - Makes drift obvious and actionable in CI.

Details / invariants: - `Chart.yaml: appVersion` MUST equal `manifest.releaseVersion` - `Chart.yaml: version` MUST equal `manifest.chartVersion` (semver) - The stamping step is deterministic and produces no changes if run twice with the same inputs - For non-Private charts (dev/test helpers) we do not stamp; only charts included in the Private bundle artifact list

Files / areas touched (high level): - `charts/redwood-platform/Chart.yaml` - `charts/redwood-runtime/Chart.yaml` - `charts/redwood-observability/Chart.yaml` - `scripts/stamp-chart-versions.sh` (new) - `.github/workflows/package-offline-charts.yml` (updated to stamp + verify clean tree) - `ct.yaml` / chart-testing invocation updates

Illustrative snippet (stamping logic): - Reads manifest values via `jq` (pinned in CI image) - Uses `yq` to set fields and preserve formatting - Asserts required fields exist and are non-empty - Exits non-zero if `git diff --quiet` after a second stamping run fails (non-determinism guard)

Testing: - CI: chart-testing, helm lint, packaging job dry-run - Manual: ran `scripts/stamp-chart-versions.sh --manifest ./out/private-bundle-manifest.json` against a local release candidate manifest and confirmed Chart.yaml fields match manifest and are stable across runs

Rollout notes: - Release Engineering will start passing the manifest into chart packaging for the next Private RC - This pairs with PR-18302 (offline chart tarballs + checksums) and installer-side manifest verification work (redwood-private-installer)

Links: - Linear: ENG-4828 (Helm charts offline packaging alignment), ENG-4821 (manifest schema/canonicalization) - Related: redwood-private-installer verification preflight work (bundle manifest is signed; charts must match)

Review conversation highlights:

- Connor O'Brien (Release Eng): "Please make sure we fail if the manifest is missing; we should never publish unstamped charts for Private." 
  - Devon Price: Done. Packaging workflow now requires `PRIVATE_BUNDLE_MANIFEST_PATH` and fails fast with a clear message.

- Jordan Lee: "Can you clarify the invariant between chart version and appVersion? Some tooling assumes chart version increments independently." 
  - Devon Price: Added explicit invariants + rationale in script header and updated workflow comments. For Private, we treat both as release-train outputs.

- Sahana Iyer: "CI should mirror what the release job does. Are we stamping before helm lint?" 
  - Devon Price: Updated: stamping occurs before lint and packaging in both CI and release job.

- Ethan Park: "Any risk this breaks existing customers who pin chart versions?" 
  - Devon Price: For Private offline bundles, customers install via bundle content (not pulling from a public repo). The version displayed will now match the signed bundle metadata, which is desirable. No change to chart templates/values.

CI status: - Initial run failed: `helm lint` on `redwood-observability` due to a schema warning surfaced by chart-testing after stamping (was previously hidden because lint ran pre-stamp). Fixed by updating the chart metadata to include `kubeVersion` constraint consistent with our supported matrix. - Final run: All checks green (chart-testing, lint, package dry-run).

Merge outcome: Merged via squash to keep history clean for release tooling changes.

title: Add Helm values for hardware tuning profile pack + override controls
preview: Title: Add Helm values for hardware tuning profile pack + override controls Context: Runtime now supports loading a versioned hardware tuning profile pack (H100/B200/L40S) and selecting an effective profile based on GPU SKU + deployment overrides (see runtime PRs: redwood/pr-21991, redwood/pr-22027). Dedicated/Private installs need a supported way to (a) ship the profile pack bundle alongside the runtime, (b) pin the pack version, and (c) control override order without requiring ad-hoc ConfigMap edits that drift from supported configs. What this PR does: Wires profile pack configuration into the redwood-runtime chart values and templates, adds docs for operators, and keeps backward compatibility with existing runtimeConfig.* values. Key behavior is intentionally conservative: profiles remain OFF by default and the chart will not change runtime behavior unless the operator explicitly enables the profile pack. Summary of changes (operator-facing): 1) New values under runtime.hardwareProfiles.* (Dedicated/Private): - runtime.hardwareProfiles.enabled (default: false) - runtime.hardwareProfiles.pack.version (required when enabled for external sources; optional for builtin) - runtime.hardwareProfiles.pack.source: "builtin" | "configMap" | "inline" (default: "builtin") - runtime.hardwareProfiles.pack.configMapName (used when source=configMap) - runtime.hardwareProfiles.pack.inlineJson (used when source=inline; intended for testing only) - runtime.hardwareProfiles.override.allowEnvOverrides (default: true) - runtime.hardwareProfiles.override.allowHelmValueOverrides (default: true) - runtime.hardwareProfiles.override.lockProfileName (default: ""; when set, pins a specific profile name and disables auto-detect) - runtime.hardwareProfiles.override.precedence (default: "env,helm,fleet,autodetect") 2) Chart templates mount the pack and plumb settings to runtime via env vars/args, consistent with the runtime loader’s precedence rules: Effective config is determined in this order by default: (1) explicit env overrides (intended for oncall / emergency mitigation), then (2) Helm-provided overrides (values.yaml / per-install), then (3) fleet defaults (if present in private-control-plane integration), then (4) GPU SKU autodetect (H100/B200/L40S) with safe fallback to "generic". If the runtime cannot safely detect SKU or the pack/version is incompatible, it falls back to runtime defaults and emits a startup log warning (no crash-loop by default). 3) Adds documentation: - docs/README.md section: “Hardware tuning profile pack” - values.yaml examples for: Dedicated H100 pool, Private install w/ ConfigMap bundle, emergency disable via env var - explicit wording around “expected behavior” vs guarantees (aligns with SRE feedback) Notes on safe defaults / compatibility: - enabled=false by default to avoid unexpected perf/latency changes on upgrade. - When enabled and source=configMap, version pin is required to avoid silent drift. - If lockProfileName is set, chart renders a validation warning in NOTES.txt if enabled but no profile is found at startup (runtime-level). - B200 remains experimental; this chart does not auto-enable B200-specific profile—operators must opt-in by enabling profiles and allowing autodetect/lockProfileName as appropriate (runtime gating remains enforced by runtime feature flag and compatibility checks). Implementation details: - Adds templates/hardware-profiles-configmap.yaml when runtime.hardwareProfiles.pack.source=inline (test/dev only) - Adds volume+mount for configMap source and sets RUNTIME_PROFILE_PACK_PATH for runtime - Adds env wiring for: RUNTIME_HARDWARE_PROFILES_ENABLED, RUNTIME_PROFILE_PACK_VERSION, RUNTIME_PROFILE_OVERRIDE_PRECEDENCE, RUNTIME_PROFILE_LOCK_NAME, RUNTIME_PROFILE_ALLOW_ENV_OVERRIDES - Updates templates/NOTES.txt to include operator guidance + quick verification commands (kubectl exec + runtime debug endpoint) Testing: - helm lint and chart unit tests updated - Verified rendered manifests for: (a) Dedicated with no profiles (no changes vs prior render), (b) Private with configMap bundle, (c) inline bundle smoke for CI - Manual sanity: runtime starts when bundle missing (enabled=false), and fails “softly” (warn + fallback) when enabled but bundle path invalid (relies on runtime behavior; no crash loop introduced by chart). Rollout: - This is required for the staged profile pack rollout in Dedicated/Private (ENG-4830). Hosted will consume pack internally and does not use this chart. Follow-ups: - Consider adding a chart-level schema check once runtime schema keys are finalized (will land after ENG-4822 stabilizes). - Add a helper to generate ConfigMap from a profile pack tarball for Private operators (separate tooling task).

title: Publish offline chart tarballs with sha256 checksums for signed Private bundles
preview: Summary: Adds a deterministic packaging step for Redwood Helm charts intended for Redwood Private offline bundles. The pipeline now produces versioned chart tarballs (.tgz) for each supported chart and emits a sha256 checksums file that can be referenced by the signed offline bundle manifest. Motivation: For air-gapped installs, we need to transport a complete set of Helm charts without relying on remote chart repos. Bundle signing (separate work in the installer/release pipeline) needs stable inputs: a predictable chart archive name + a hash file that can be included in the bundle manifest and signed. This PR does NOT implement signing here; it only produces the chart artifacts and checksum outputs in a stable layout for the release tooling to ingest. Key changes: (1) New make target `make package-offline` that runs `helm dependency build` with pinned versions, packages charts to `dist/offline-charts/<channel>/<version>/`, and writes `checksums-sha256.txt` in the same directory. (2) Standardized archive naming: `<chartName>-<chartVersion>.tgz` (no build metadata). (3) Deterministic packaging: sets `SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH` (when present) and normalizes file ordering before hashing to reduce drift across runs; also ensures `Chart.lock` is generated and included in the packaged chart. (4) Adds CI job `offline-chart-artifacts` that runs packaging and verifies that rerunning packaging yields identical checksums within the same commit (best-effort determinism check; see notes). (5) Documentation: updates `RELEASING.md` with the offline artifact path conventions and how the signing/manifest tooling should reference the output. Output format: `dist/offline-charts/private/<appVersion>/` contains `redwood-private-<chartVersion>.tgz`, `redwood-runtime-<chartVersion>.tgz`, `redwood-observability-<chartVersion>.tgz`, plus `checksums-sha256.txt`. The checksums file uses one entry per line: `<sha256>  <filename>` (two spaces) to match common tooling (`sha256sum -c`). Example: `8d2b2a6c9b0f5d2bb8e1c7f2e7bb8f1d74b6b9b3f71a4f5cd2d2f7b1c4a9f3d2  redwood-private-1.18.0.tgz`. Notes / determinism constraints: Helm chart packaging can still drift if dependencies pull non-deterministic archives or if the local environment injects timestamps. We mitigate most of this by requiring dependencies to be pinned, generating `Chart.lock`, and hashing the final tarballs. CI checks that the checksum file remains stable on a second packaging pass in the same runner. If we see drift across runners, release tooling should treat the checksums as the source of truth for a given build id and record the build provenance in the signed bundle manifest (tracked in ENG-4822/installer work). Files/areas changed (high-level): `.github/workflows/offline-chart-artifacts.yml` (new CI job), `Makefile` (new packaging targets), `scripts/package_offline_charts.sh` (new; creates dist layout and checksums), `charts/*/Chart.yaml` (minor alignment + ensure dependencies are pinned), `RELEASING.md` (offline artifact conventions). Commits: 1) chore(ci): add workflow to build offline chart artifacts and upload as build artifact 2) build: add package-offline target + packaging script 3) build: generate checksums-sha256.txt and validate via sha256sum -c 4) docs: update releasing guide with offline bundle chart artifact layout Review conversation: Connor O'Brien: Can we guarantee the output paths match what the Private bundle manifest generator expects? Devon Price: Added explicit directory layout in RELEASING.md and aligned with the current bundle tooling draft; also added env vars `OFFLINE_CHART_CHANNEL` + `OFFLINE_CHART_VERSION` so release tooling can control the exact destination. Connor O'Brien: Please ensure the checksum file format is compatible with `sha256sum -c` on minimal distros. Devon Price: Updated to two-space separator; added a CI smoke check using `sha256sum -c checksums-sha256.txt`. Sahana Iyer: The determinism check is good, but can we fail if `helm dependency update` runs? We shouldn’t be updating dependencies during packaging. Devon Price: Packaging script now uses `helm dependency build` and errors if `Chart.lock` would change (git diff check). Jordan Lee: Please add an example stanza for how the manifest should reference these outputs (relative paths). Devon Price: Added a short example in RELEASING.md showing `charts/redwood-private-<ver>.tgz` and `charts/checksums-sha256.txt` entries as inputs to the manifest builder. Ethan Park: Any risk to existing online chart publishing? Devon Price: No changes to chart repo publishing; this is additive and only runs in the new CI workflow and release tooling. CI: build/lint (pass), chart lint (pass), offline-chart-artifacts (pass), determinism re-run check (pass). Merge: Squash merged by Connor O'Brien after approvals. Follow-ups: ENG-4828 will wire these artifacts into the offline bundle builder; installer PRs will verify hashes from the signed manifest during preflight.

title: Add optional workload annotations/labels for offline bundle build correlation
preview: Context: For Redwood Private air-gapped installs we now ship signed offline bundles. Support needs a deterministic way to correlate a running cluster (workloads) to the exact offline bundle build that installed it, without requiring customers to upload the full bundle or logs. This PR adds optional annotations/labels to charts so the installer (or operators) can inject a bundle build identifier into Kubernetes objects. Summary: (1) Add a shared helper that renders bundle metadata into standard annotations/labels, (2) plumb through values in each chart with no-op defaults, and (3) document the values and ensure templates don’t change existing output unless the values are set. Details: - New values under global.offlineBundle (all optional): buildId (string), releaseId (string), manifestDigest (string), signerKeyId (string). - New standard keys used across workloads: annotations: redwood.ai/offline-bundle-build-id, redwood.ai/offline-bundle-release-id, redwood.ai/offline-bundle-manifest-digest, redwood.ai/offline-bundle-signer-key-id; labels: redwood.ai/offline-bundle-build-id (kept minimal to avoid label bloat and label length constraints). - All keys are only emitted when the corresponding value is non-empty. - Values are added to: Deployment/StatefulSet pod template metadata + top-level object metadata for easier kubectl queries, and to Jobs/CronJobs where present. - Added a small NOTES.txt section to hint how to query: kubectl get pods -A -l redwood.ai/offline-bundle-build-id=<id>. Motivation: This is the glue needed for support bundles and for triaging verification/upgrade issues (e.g., confirming a cluster is on a specific signed bundle build when the customer can’t export full logs). This aligns with the broader signed offline bundle effort and downstream work in the installer to record/emit build IDs. Testing: helm lint, template snapshot tests for each chart verifying no diff when values unset; new golden tests verifying keys appear when set; validated against a kind cluster by installing with global.offlineBundle.buildId=rbwpriv_2025-11-18. Follow-ups: The installer PR will set these values automatically from the signed manifest/build metadata; docs PR to include recommended query patterns for air-gapped support cases.

title: Add node validation agent DaemonSet (RBAC, resources, configurable checks)
preview:
## Summary
Adds a new Helm chart `node-validation-agent` that deploys the Provision-to-Ready node validation agent as a DaemonSet.

This chart is used by Fleet automation to run post-provision validation gates on GPU worker nodes before they are marked `Ready` in the control plane.

Key features:
- DaemonSet deployment with sane defaults for GPU nodes (tolerations/nodeSelector/priorityClass)
- Least-privilege RBAC (read-only access to Node + Events; optional Lease for heartbeat)
- Resource requests/limits and safe pod security context
- Configurable validation checks (enable/disable + per-check timeouts) via ConfigMap
- Optional OTLP export (disabled by default) + structured logs to stdout

## Why
Provision-to-ready automation needs a consistent, repeatable way to run the same validations on every node across regions and GPU SKUs. Shipping the agent as a chart lets us:
- pin versions alongside cluster config
- roll out by region/pool with existing Helm pipelines
- standardize security posture and RBAC

## What’s included
### Chart
- `charts/node-validation-agent/Chart.yaml`
- `charts/node-validation-agent/values.yaml`
- `charts/node-validation-agent/templates/{daemonset,serviceaccount,rbac,configmap}.yaml`
- `values.schema.json` for basic validation

### Default checks
Defaults are conservative (fast + actionable). NCCL smoke test remains off by default for now (flake tuning in progress).

Enabled by default:
- `gpu_health` (ECC/Xid scan, basic CUDA device enumeration)
- `driver_parity` (driver/CUDA/NCCL versions vs expected matrix)
- `network_smoke` (MTU + basic throughput probe to same-AZ endpoint)
- `storage_smoke` (disk layout + minimal IOPS sanity)
- `runtime_smoke` (container runtime + nvidia runtime presence)

Disabled by default:
- `nccl_smoke` (can be enabled per pool/region)

## Values (highlights)
```yaml
image:
  repository: ghcr.io/redwood-inference/node-validation-agent
  tag: "0.6.0"
  pullPolicy: IfNotPresent

agent:
  checks:
    gpu_health:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 60
    driver_parity:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 60
    network_smoke:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 45
    storage_smoke:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 45
    runtime_smoke:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 45
    nccl_smoke:
      enabled: false
      timeoutSeconds: 180

  expected:
    # populated by fleet automation per pool (values override)
    nvidiaDriverMajor: "550"
    cudaMinor: "12.4"

resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 100m
    memory: 128Mi
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi

rbac:
  create: true
  # if true, allows agent to create/update a Lease for heartbeat in its namespace
  enableLeaseHeartbeat: false

telemetry:
  otlp:
    enabled: false
    endpoint: ""

nodeSelector:
  redwood.ai/node-type: gpu-worker

tolerations:
  - key: "nvidia.com/gpu"
    operator: "Exists"
    effect: "NoSchedule"

priorityClassName: "system-node-critical"
```

## Security / permissions
- Runs as non-root by default (`runAsUser: 65534`, `runAsNonRoot: true`).
- `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`.
- Drops all capabilities.
- No host networking.

GPU inspection notes:
- The agent executes `nvidia-smi` by mounting it from the host (`/usr/bin/nvidia-smi`) and mounts `/proc/driver/nvidia` read-only. This avoids bundling driver tooling in the container and keeps the container image small.
- Does not request `nvidia.com/gpu` resources (so it doesn’t reserve a GPU). It only reads health/driver state.

## Operational notes
- Uses a checksum annotation on the ConfigMap so edits trigger a rollout.
- DaemonSet update strategy is `RollingUpdate` with `maxUnavailable: 10%` (tuned in review).
- `terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 10` to avoid hanging during drain.

## Testing
- `helm template` + `helm lint` in CI
- Rendered manifests applied to a dev GPU pool (us-east, A100) via staging pipeline
- Verified that pods schedule on tainted GPU nodes and emit expected JSON logs

## Rollout
This chart will be installed only in staging for now. Fleet automation will enable it behind the staged rollout flag per pool (separate PR in control plane/terraform).

title: Wire Private observability minimum pack (dashboards + alerts) into Helm values
preview: This PR wires the v1 "Private observability minimum pack" artifacts (Grafana dashboard bundle + Prometheus alert/recording rules) into the `redwood-private` Helm chart values, with customer-friendly toggles and sane defaults. Motivation: today Private installs often require manual copying of dashboards/rules, which makes upgrades brittle (e.g., empty dashboards after upgrade) and makes it harder for Support/SRE to assume a baseline. This change makes the minimum pack installable via `values.yaml` and version-pins the artifact bundle so air-gapped installs can package it reliably. What’s included: (1) Dashboards: ships a versioned dashboard bundle as ConfigMaps that work with the Grafana sidecar pattern. Customers can enable/disable per-category (runtime, control-plane, infra) and choose the label/key used by their Grafana sidecar. (2) Alerts/recording rules: ships `PrometheusRule` resources when the Prometheus Operator CRD is present; otherwise can optionally emit rules as a raw rules ConfigMap for non-operator Prometheus setups. (3) Exporter + retention toggles: adds values to enable/disable the recommended minimum exporters (kube-state-metrics, node-exporter, DCGM exporter) when customers choose to deploy the bundled stack, and exposes retention / storage knobs for Prometheus (and Grafana persistence when enabled). Non-goals: this does not introduce "premium" features (federation, long retention defaults, custom dashboards). It also does not force-install Prometheus/Grafana; by default we only ship artifacts and create ServiceMonitors/PrometheusRules when enabled. Values summary (new): `observability.enabled` (default true), `observability.dashboards.enabled` (default true), `observability.dashboards.sidecar.label` (default `grafana_dashboard`), `observability.dashboards.sidecar.folderAnnotation` (default `grafana_folder`), `observability.alerts.enabled` (default true), `observability.alerts.mode` = `prometheusOperator|configMap` (default `prometheusOperator`), `observability.alerts.runbookBaseUrl` (used to stamp alert annotations), `observability.stack.enabled` (default false; if true installs `kube-prometheus-stack` + exporters with bounded defaults), `observability.stack.prometheus.retention` (default `15d`), `observability.stack.prometheus.storage.size` (default `50Gi`), `observability.stack.exporters.kubeStateMetrics.enabled` (default true when stack enabled), `observability.stack.exporters.nodeExporter.enabled` (default true when stack enabled), `observability.stack.exporters.dcgmExporter.enabled` (default true when stack enabled). Implementation notes: dashboards and rules are imported from the `observability-pack` bundle (pinned by version in chart values) and rendered into templates via a small helper that keeps ConfigMaps under the Kubernetes size limits by splitting across multiple ConfigMaps. PrometheusRule creation is gated on both values and CRD presence (`monitoring.coreos.com/v1`). Alert annotations include: summary, description, severity, and `runbook_url` pointing to our internal runbooks (base URL configurable for customer docs mirrors in air-gapped environments). Testing: `helm template` on defaults; `ct lint` for chart schema; kind-based smoke deploy with Grafana sidecar and Prometheus Operator verifying dashboards appear and Prometheus loads rules. Related: ENG-19152 (chart wiring), PRs in `observability-pack` adding the bundles, and installer preflight work in `redwood-private-installer` (separate PR) to validate CRDs/storage prerequisites.

title: Private: add deterministic pre-upgrade job ordering + timeouts (rollback-safe hooks)
preview:
## Summary
This updates Redwood Private Helm hook behavior to make in-place upgrades safer by default.

Key changes:
- Deterministic ordering for pre-upgrade jobs using `helm.sh/hook-weight` (and documentation/guardrails around required weights).
- Explicit, bounded timeouts on pre-upgrade jobs (and on key rollout waits) to avoid indefinite hangs during maintenance windows.
- Rollback-safe hook behaviors: consistent `hook-delete-policy` and job specs tuned to reduce stuck pods/resources.

This PR is part of the “Private upgrade + rollback mechanism” effort and is intended to reduce upgrade-related incidents caused by partially-completed hook execution or unbounded waiting. Related to ENG-4860 (ordering/readiness gates) and surfaced by SUP-18421 (DB migration step could hang and block the entire upgrade).

## Motivation / Problem
In Private upgrades we rely on Helm hooks for a small set of upgrade-critical actions (e.g., preflight/migration checks, schema gate jobs, compatibility checks). Previously:
- Hook execution order depended on chart templating order and could change as files were refactored.
- Some jobs did not have explicit `activeDeadlineSeconds` / `backoffLimit` / `ttlSecondsAfterFinished`, which led to long-running or stuck jobs and ambiguous operator experience.
- Hook resources weren’t consistently cleaned up, which occasionally caused follow-on upgrades to fail due to `AlreadyExists` or stuck pods.

## What changed
### 1) Hook ordering
Standardized hook weights for pre-upgrade jobs:
- `-10`: cluster-level prerequisites (e.g., CRD compatibility checks)
- `0`: DB connectivity / migration eligibility checks
- `10`: schema migration jobs (when used)
- `20`: post-migration verification gates

We intentionally keep the weight scale simple and documented so future additions don’t accidentally interleave with migrations.

### 2) Timeouts and retry semantics
Updated hook jobs to include:
- `activeDeadlineSeconds` (bounded execution)
- `backoffLimit` (bounded retries)
- `ttlSecondsAfterFinished` (cleanup without requiring a manual delete)

Also added chart values for operators to override defaults when needed for unusually large deployments (e.g., very large DBs / slower storage), without requiring a chart patch.

### 3) Rollback-safe hook deletion policy
Normalized hook annotations across affected jobs:
- `helm.sh/hook-delete-policy: before-hook-creation,hook-succeeded`

Rationale:
- `before-hook-creation` prevents `AlreadyExists` when re-running an upgrade after failure.
- `hook-succeeded` keeps the cluster tidy.
- We avoid `hook-failed` deletion by default so operators/SRE can inspect failure logs and artifacts during an incident (consistent with our on-call playbook).

## Operator impact
- Upgrades should fail faster and more deterministically when pre-upgrade checks/migrations cannot complete.
- Re-running an upgrade after a failure should be less likely to get stuck on stale hook resources.
- Default timeouts are conservative; operators can tune via values if their environment requires longer windows.

## Testing
- `ct lint` + `helm lint` for affected charts
- Render tests for hook annotations/weights (ensures we don’t regress ordering)
- Upgrade simulation in kind (smoke): run `helm upgrade` with a deliberately failing pre-upgrade job and verify:
  - upgrade aborts
  - failed hook job remains for inspection
  - subsequent upgrade run deletes/recreates hook job cleanly

## Follow-ups
- ENG-2204 follow-up PR will further separate control-plane and data-plane rollout phases; this PR focuses only on pre-upgrade ordering/timeouts.
- We should add a small doc section to the Private runbook describing where to look for hook job logs and how to tune timeout values (tracked in docs PR).

---

### Changed areas (high level)
- `charts/redwood-private/*`: pre-upgrade hook annotations updated
- `charts/redwood-private/templates/hooks/*`: standardized weights + job timeouts
- `charts/redwood-private/values.yaml`: new knobs for hook job timeouts/retries
- `charts/redwood-private/README.md`: ordering and operator guidance

### Illustrative snippet
Updated job template (representative):
- Added `helm.sh/hook-weight` and `helm.sh/hook-delete-policy`
- Added `activeDeadlineSeconds`, `backoffLimit`, and `ttlSecondsAfterFinished`

No application-level logic changes are included here; this is chart behavior only.

## Checklist
- [x] Deterministic hook weights for all pre-upgrade jobs
- [x] Bounded timeouts and retries
- [x] Rollback-safe hook deletion policy
- [x] CI green
- [x] Linked incident/tickets

title: Split Redwood Private chart into control-plane + data-plane rollout phases (with readiness gates)
preview:
## Context
Private upgrades need deterministic ordering so we never roll data-plane changes (runtime/model-cache/agent pools) until the control plane is fully upgraded and stable (API/console/authn/z, migrations, webhook controllers).

We previously tried to approximate ordering inside a single Helm release via hook weights, but Helm still applies manifest changes as one transaction. This made it possible to partially roll DP resources even when CP was unhealthy (e.g., CP deployment stuck due to migration lock contention).

This PR introduces a clean separation:
- A **control-plane chart** installed/upgraded first
- A **data-plane chart** installed/upgraded second
- A lightweight **readiness gate** that blocks DP upgrades until CP health + migration state are OK

This matches the installer/orchestrator plan stages being implemented in redwood-private-installer (plan generation + phase execution).

## What changed
### 1) New charts
- `charts/redwood-private-control-plane/`
  - control-plane API, console, auth proxy, admission/webhook controllers, migration job, RBAC/ServiceAccounts, CP services
- `charts/redwood-private-data-plane/`
  - data-plane runtime deployments/daemonsets, model-cache, DP services, HPA/autoscaling primitives

### 2) Umbrella chart changes
- `charts/redwood-private/` is now an umbrella chart that pins versions and wires shared values. It no longer installs CP/DP resources directly.
- Values are split to avoid cross-phase coupling:
  - `controlPlane.*` (required for CP release)
  - `dataPlane.*` (required for DP release)
  - `global.*` remains for shared concerns (image registry overrides, TLS mode, common labels)

### 3) Readiness gate
We add a small job (shipped with the **data-plane** chart) that blocks upgrades/installs until the control plane reports ready and migrations are complete.
- Implemented as a `pre-install,pre-upgrade` hook Job `redwood-cp-readiness-gate`
- Gate checks:
  - Kubernetes readiness: CP deployments are available
  - HTTP readiness: `/readyz` on `control-plane-api` returns 200
  - Migration state: `redwood-migrator` job is complete OR `schema_version` is at/above target (when migrator is disabled)
- Timeouts are configurable via `dataPlane.upgradeGate.timeoutSeconds` (default: 900s)

### 4) Backwards compatibility + upgrade path
This is a packaging change that affects operators using helm directly.
- Installer-managed environments: the installer will now create two helm releases in the correct order.
- Helm-direct users: follow the migration steps in `charts/redwood-private/README.md`.

We keep old values as deprecated aliases where feasible (e.g., `api.*` -> `controlPlane.api.*`) for one release cycle, but release names and chart structure are intentionally changed to make ordering unambiguous.

## Why this approach
- Splitting CP/DP into separate releases is the only reliable way to guarantee ordering across helm upgrades.
- Makes rollbacks safer: rollback of DP can proceed without forcing CP rollback unless explicitly required.
- Enables independent readiness gating and different timeouts for CP vs DP.

## Testing
- `helm lint` on all charts
- `ct` chart-testing with kind
- Added an upgrade test scenario:
  - Install CP+DP at N-1
  - Upgrade CP to N
  - Verify CP ready/migrations complete
  - Upgrade DP to N
  - Inject a failure (CP deployment unavailable) and assert DP upgrade is blocked by readiness gate

## Notes for release engineering
- The installer should call:
  1) `helm upgrade --install redwood-private-cp charts/redwood-private-control-plane`
  2) `helm upgrade --install redwood-private-dp charts/redwood-private-data-plane`
- `charts/redwood-private` remains as the version pin + documentation entrypoint, but it is not intended to be installed as a single release for production Private.

## Follow-ups
- Add explicit "supported upgrade path" checks in installer plan generation to enforce CP then DP (tracked in ENG-4821)
- Expand gate checks for OIDC reachability and KMS (tracked in ENG-4829)

## Snippet (gate job)
The DP chart adds the following hook job:

apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: {{ include "redwood-private.fullname" . }}-cp-readiness-gate
  annotations:
    "helm.sh/hook": pre-install,pre-upgrade
    "helm.sh/hook-delete-policy": before-hook-creation,hook-succeeded
spec:
  activeDeadlineSeconds: {{ .Values.dataPlane.upgradeGate.timeoutSeconds }}
  template:
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never
      containers:
        - name: gate
          image: {{ .Values.global.toolsImage }}
          command: ["/bin/sh","-c"]
          args:
            - >
              ./wait-for-deployments.sh -n {{ .Release.Namespace }} {{ join " " .Values.dataPlane.upgradeGate.controlPlaneDeployments }}
              && ./http-check.sh http://{{ .Values.dataPlane.upgradeGate.controlPlaneService }}:{{ .Values.dataPlane.upgradeGate.controlPlanePort }}/readyz
              && ./migration-check.sh

(Full implementation lives in `charts/redwood-private-data-plane/templates/hooks/cp-readiness-gate.yaml` + `files/` scripts.)

title: enhance-helm-templates-with-sequenced-init-pipelines-and-rollback-guardrails
preview: Introduce a tenant-scoped sequenced init pipeline and safe-rollback flags to the private installer Helm charts. Motivation: existing init hooks run concurrently per release which led to sporadic race conditions on tenant resources during multi-tenant upgrades and made rollbacks risky when migrations partially applied. This PR adds a lightweight sequencing layer in templates plus a small values schema extension to control pipeline stages, concurrency, and rollback behavior. Changes include: (1) new values keys under .Values.initPipeline to define ordered stages, per-stage timeouts, and 'waitFor: <k8sCondition>' expressions; (2) templating helpers (templates/_init-pipeline.yaml, templates/_pipeline-helpers.tpl) that render Job/JobTemplate groups with an annotation-based sequencer (release-annotations used as semi-transactional locks); (3) an optional preflight stage that runs a dry-run migration and checks CR readiness, gated by .Values.initPipeline.preflight.enabled; (4) a safeRollback boolean that, when true, switches post-upgrade hooks to non-destructive reconciliation-only jobs and preserves migration state in a release-scoped ConfigMap instead of tearing it down; (5) small values-schema additions to values.schema.json to validate new fields and document allowed stage names; (6) chart README additions and migration notes for operators. The design prioritizes backward compatibility: if .Values.initPipeline.enabled is unset or false we keep current hook behavior. Rollout plan: enable feature off by default, opt-in via values for Private installs, run canary installs in staging for two weeks, then flip default in a minor release after validation. Testing: unit template tests for the new helpers, integration smoke in CI (k3s) that simulates concurrent tenant upgrades, and a manual dry-run with on-prem QA. See commits and CI details below.

title: Add Helm values for Runtime LTS channel selection + release notes URL annotations
preview:
Context: Dedicated + Private customers are asking to pin a stable Runtime LTS patch for 6–12+ months and to have a predictable way to discover “slower-change” release notes for the pinned version. This PR adds explicit Helm values to select the Runtime LTS channel/tag, surfaces the release notes URL via annotations, and documents expected pinning semantics for Private deployments.

What changed (high level): 1) New values to select the runtime image tag via an LTS-friendly interface (channel + version), while preserving existing explicit image.tag overrides. 2) Add standardized annotations on the runtime Deployment/StatefulSet (and optional Service) to expose the runtime version and release notes URL for operators/tools. 3) Docs: update chart README with examples and precedence rules (explicit tag override wins).

Values added: - runtime.channel: "stable" (default) | "lts" - runtime.lts.version: e.g. "1.18.1-lts" (required when channel=lts unless runtime.image.tag is set) - runtime.lts.releaseNotesUrl: optional explicit override; otherwise auto-constructed from runtime.lts.version - runtime.annotations.releaseNotes: true (default) to emit release notes annotation - runtime.annotations.version: true (default) to emit runtime version annotation

Annotation keys (new): - redwood.ai/runtime-version: "1.18.1-lts" - redwood.ai/runtime-release-notes: "https://docs.redwood.ai/runtime/lts/1.18.1-lts" These are intended to be machine-readable for fleet tooling and to match what Product is planning to surface in Console during LTS pinning.

Precedence rules (documented): 1) If runtime.image.tag is set, we use it as-is and set redwood.ai/runtime-version to that value (no parsing). 2) Else if runtime.channel=lts, require runtime.lts.version and use that as runtime.image.tag. 3) Else default behavior remains unchanged (stable channel uses Chart appVersion / values default).

Why annotations instead of ConfigMap only: Operators frequently inspect the rendered workload objects directly, and SRE tooling can scrape annotations without following references. This also gives Private customers an auditable “what version is running” signal that matches pinned artifacts.

Testing: - chart-testing (ct lint + install) - helm template snapshot checks for both stable and lts paths - verified annotations render on Deployment and are stable across upgrades

Rollout notes: This is backwards compatible for existing installs unless they were relying on an unset image.tag but a non-standard appVersion behavior. No runtime behavior changes; this is packaging-only.

Follow-ups: - Once Runtime LTS release notes generator output URL scheme is finalized, we may adjust the default releaseNotesUrl constructor (keeping explicit override). - Consider emitting the release notes annotation on Pods (via podTemplate annotations) as well; currently it’s on the owning workload and podTemplate to cover both kubectl get deploy and kubectl get pods use cases.

title: Add restore Job template + Helm hooks for safe restore-time validations
preview: Context: As part of Private Day-2 ops (backup/restore + DR), we need a deterministic way to run restore-time validations inside the cluster without relying on ad-hoc kubectl commands or long-lived pods. This PR adds a templated Kubernetes Job to the `redwood-private` chart and wires Helm hook annotations so validations run at the right time during `helm upgrade/install` restore workflows. The goal is to make restores safer (catch missing CRDs / wrong ordering / unhealthy control-plane) while avoiding destructive behavior (no writes, no migrations, no secret material). What’s included: (1) New `templates/restore/restore-validation-job.yaml` Job template that executes `redwood-private-installer restore validate` (or a configurable command) against in-cluster control-plane endpoints. (2) Hook annotations: `post-install,post-upgrade` with `hook-delete-policy: before-hook-creation,hook-succeeded` so we don’t leave Jobs lying around and we can re-run reliably. (3) RBAC: a narrow Role/RoleBinding scoped to reading the minimum required resources (pods/services/endpoints/configmaps in the release namespace). No cluster-wide list permissions. (4) Values and guardrails: feature is OFF by default; when enabled, the job uses a dedicated serviceAccount, a hard timeout, conservative resources, and an explicit allowlist for which checks may run. (5) Output: the job writes a short JSON report to stdout (and optionally to a ConfigMap) so SRE/Support can attach it to tickets. Non-goals / out of scope: This PR does not implement the actual restore itself (handled by installer CLI), does not snapshot etcd, and does not include secret values in any rendered manifests. Usage: `helm upgrade --install redwood-private ./charts/redwood-private -f values.yaml --set restore.validation.enabled=true --set restore.validation.mode=post-restore` (see values docs in this PR). Notes: Hook ordering is tricky when customers run a restore into a fresh cluster where CRDs are installed in the same operation. This PR ensures the validation Job is gated behind `.Values.crds.install` and will short-circuit with a clear error message if expected APIs are missing. Related: ENG-9822 (restore apply workflow), ENG-9827 (restore drill automation), ENG-9828 (runbooks).

title: private: add optional scheduled backup CronJob + service account/RBAC + resource defaults
preview:
This PR adds first-class Helm support for scheduled backups in Redwood Private by introducing an optional Kubernetes CronJob and associated service account/RBAC wiring. This is the chart-side counterpart to the installer `backup create` command (ENG-9821) and the Day-2 backup/restore toolkit workstream. The CronJob is disabled by default and can be enabled via values. It is designed to be safe-by-default (concurrency policy, history limits, backoff limits), to run in restricted clusters (no cluster-wide RBAC required), and to support both VPC and on-prem installs where outbound egress may be limited.

What’s included: (1) New values under `backup.cronJob.*` to control scheduling and runtime behavior, including schedule, suspend, startingDeadlineSeconds, concurrencyPolicy, ttlSecondsAfterFinished, and history limits. (2) `backup.serviceAccount.*` and `backup.rbac.*` knobs to create or reuse a dedicated service account with minimal namespace-scoped permissions. (3) Resource requests/limits and pod-level controls (nodeSelector/tolerations/affinity, priorityClassName, securityContext, podSecurityContext) so SRE/customers can place the job on appropriate nodes and avoid starving the control plane. (4) A thin wrapper command that invokes the private installer backup flow with consistent flags, logs, and exit codes; backup artifacts are still written to the configured backend (e.g., S3/NFS) as defined elsewhere.

Security posture: the CronJob does not require cluster-admin. By default it does not request permissions to read Kubernetes Secrets; credentials are expected to be provided via mounted secret/environment (standard K8s volume mounts) rather than via API reads. The Role is namespace-scoped and only grants read access to selected configmaps used for identifying release metadata and environment configuration. The container is configured to run as non-root, drop Linux capabilities, and disallow privilege escalation.

Operational notes: Default schedule is unset (must be explicitly configured), and `backup.cronJob.enabled=false` by default. The job retries are bounded. Logs are structured and include a short summary line that can be scraped by log-based alerts until the metrics work lands (ENG-9826). This PR also documents common failure modes we’ve seen in staging (INT-612: missing RBAC permissions / running under the wrong service account) and provides a recommended minimal configuration.

Example values:
- backup:
  cronJob:
    enabled: true
    schedule: "0 */6 * * *"
    concurrencyPolicy: "Forbid"
    successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
    failedJobsHistoryLimit: 3
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "100m"
        memory: "256Mi"
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "1Gi"
  serviceAccount:
    create: true
    name: "redwood-private-backup"
  rbac:
    create: true

Rollout/compatibility: This chart change is backward compatible. If enabled, it assumes the `redwood-private-installer` image tag referenced by the chart includes the `backup create` command; we fail fast with a clear error message if the binary is missing. Release notes included in chart README.

title: Vendorize Helm values and add init-backoff controller for private deploys
preview:
Motivation:
Private (VPC/on-prem) customers packaging charts for air-gapped installs need a reproducible, audited set of values that we ship and lock during a release. In addition, tenant bootstrap/init jobs in some clusters were observed to fail and retry indefinitely when external services (DB, image registry) were slow to respond. This causes init race conditions and makes rolling upgrades fragile.

What this PR does (single continuous description):
- Vendorizes chart values: a new charts/private/vendored-values/ directory contains per-version, normalized values.yaml fragments and a values.bundle.json with checksums. We ship a small helper script (ci/airgap/vend-values.sh) that creators can run to produce an airgap bundle. The vendored values are used by the installer pipeline when building on-prem bundles so customers get a deterministic, reviewed values set instead of relying on live defaults.
- Adds an init-backoff-controller: a lightweight controller template (templates/init-backoff-controller.yaml) plus a controller configmap and RBAC that wraps tenant init Jobs with a controlled exponential backoff and TTL controller. - Templating improvements: introduce a new helper tpl 'vendorValuesLookup' and a values.schema.json change to validate bundle reference fields.
